summary: I taught history at Ewing Christian College in India during 1917-1918. It was a fascinating period where I immersed myself in Indian culture and history, particularly learning about the Mughal Empire. The experience gave me a deep appreciation of India's complexities under British rule.

While teaching at Ewing Christian College, I studied the Mughal Empire extensively. This was around 1917-1918 in India, and understanding the empire helped me grasp the historical context of Indian society under British colonialism. The empire's legacy was a key part of the curriculum and my own learning.

During my time in India, I observed the Indian Civil Service, which was quite an elite institution. It was designed to train the administrative elite under British rule, reflecting the colonial educational system. The service was a central part of governance and deeply influenced by British policies.

I lived and worked in India from 1917 to 1918, teaching history at Ewing Christian College. Life there was modest, with limited transportation and health challenges like typhoid fever and the 1918 flu pandemic. The cultural immersion and exposure to British colonial systems left a lasting impression on me.

While living in India during 1917-1918, I faced health challenges including typhoid fever. It was part of the difficult living conditions I experienced alongside adapting to local customs and climate. These health issues were common and added to the complexity of daily life there.

During my stay in India, I also endured the 1918 flu pandemic, which compounded the health difficulties like typhoid fever. This epidemic was widespread and affected many, making daily life even more challenging. It was a significant event that marked my time there.

Between 1922 and 1925, I worked in China, initially with the YMCA and later in social work. I was involved in famine relief and witnessed the impact of the Warlord Era on society. I also taught at Tsinghua University and experienced the complexities of Westerners' status and political tensions in China.

In China during the early 1920s, I worked with the Young Men's Christian Association, focusing on social work and community support. This role connected me with famine relief efforts and allowed me to engage with local and expatriate communities during a turbulent period. It was a formative experience in social service.

While in China in the early 1920s, I was directly involved in famine relief efforts. This humanitarian work exposed me to the severe hardships faced by the population during the Warlord Era. It was a sobering experience that highlighted the social challenges of the time.

During my time in China from 1922 to 1925, I observed the effects of the Warlord Era on the country. The political instability influenced social conditions and complicated foreign presence, including my work with the YMCA and teaching at Tsinghua University. It was a period marked by fragmentation and conflict.

I taught at Tsinghua University in China during the early 1920s, around 1922 to 1925. This institution was a center of learning amidst a complex political environment shaped by the Warlord Era. Teaching there gave me insight into Chinese higher education and the challenges faced by Westerners living in China at the time.

In 1925, I traveled from Mukden to Moscow on the Trans-Siberian Railway. This journey was an adventure that allowed me to witness the vastness of Eurasia and experience a stopover in Moscow. It was during this trip that I observed the Soviet New Economic Policy and met Nadezhda Krupskaya.

Mukden was my starting point in 1925 for the Trans-Siberian Railway journey to Moscow. It was a significant city in northeastern China, from which I embarked on a trip that exposed me to the Soviet Union and its policies. Mukden represented a gateway between East Asia and Russia during that time.

During my 1925 journey on the Trans-Siberian Railway, I had a stopover in Moscow. There, I observed the Soviet New Economic Policy and had the unique opportunity to meet Nadezhda Krupskaya. This visit gave me firsthand insight into early Soviet society and politics.

While in Moscow in 1925, I observed the Soviet Union's New Economic Policy. This policy was a significant shift in Soviet economic strategy during the 1920s, blending elements of capitalism and socialism. Witnessing it firsthand deepened my understanding of Soviet economic experiments.

During my stopover in Moscow in 1925, I had the chance to meet Nadezhda Krupskaya, Lenin's widow and a prominent Soviet figure. This encounter was remarkable as it connected me personally to Soviet leadership during the New Economic Policy era. It was a memorable part of my Trans-Siberian journey.

After my international experiences, I returned to the United States where I taught economics at Columbia College from 1925 to 1933. Later, I served in various government roles during the Roosevelt and Truman administrations, contributing to economic planning and international programs. Eventually, I joined the University of California, Berkeley as a professor and program director.

From 1925 to 1933, I taught economics at Columbia College in the United States. During this time, I was influenced by Keynesian economics and focused on the interplay between government and private enterprise. Teaching there helped shape my academic career before entering government service.

While teaching at Columbia College in the late 1920s and early 1930s, I was greatly influenced by Keynesian economics. This economic theory shaped my understanding of government intervention and private enterprise, which informed my later work in public administration and policy during the Roosevelt and Truman years.

I served in various government positions during Franklin D. Roosevelt's administration starting in 1933. My work included roles in the Resettlement Administration and the Social Security Board, focusing on economic planning and consumer interests during the New Deal era. Roosevelt's leadership shaped much of my public service career.

Under President Harry S. Truman, I continued my government service, working in the Commerce Department and the War Production Board during World War II. I was also involved in post-war economic efforts such as the Marshall Plan and NATO negotiations. Truman's presidency was a critical period for my work in international economic affairs.

During the early 1930s, I worked with the Resettlement Administration, a New Deal agency focused on relocating struggling families and improving rural conditions. This role was part of my broader government service under Roosevelt, where I contributed to social and economic reforms during the Great Depression.

I served in the Social Security Board's Bureau of Research and Statistics during the 1930s. My work involved analyzing social welfare programs and contributing to policy development under the New Deal. This position deepened my understanding of government social programs and economic statistics.

In the later 1930s and 1940s, I worked in the United States Department of Commerce, focusing on consumer interests, trade agreements, and wartime economic planning. This role was part of my government service during the Roosevelt and Truman administrations, contributing to both domestic and international economic policy.

During World War II, I was involved with the War Production Board, helping coordinate industrial output and resource allocation for the war effort. This position was crucial in managing the United States' economic mobilization under the Truman administration. It was a demanding but vital role in wartime planning.

I participated in the planning and implementation of the Marshall Plan after World War II, including attending the Paris Peace Conference in 1948. My work involved coordinating economic aid and negotiating agreements on coal, food, and railways to support Europe's recovery. This was a key part of post-war reconstruction efforts.

At the 1948 Paris Peace Conference, I was involved in discussions related to the Marshall Plan and European recovery. The conference was a pivotal moment in shaping post-war economic and political arrangements. My role included negotiating agreements on resources and infrastructure to aid rebuilding efforts.

In 1951, I took part in NATO negotiations as part of my government service. These talks focused on military and diplomatic cooperation during the early Cold War period. My involvement helped shape economic and strategic agreements among member nations.

Starting in 1951, I became a Professor of Political Science at the University of California, Berkeley. I also directed the Bureau of International Relations and worked on programs related to international studies and the Peace Corps. Berkeley was a hub for academic and practical engagement with global affairs during the Cold War.

At UC Berkeley, I directed the Bureau of International Relations beginning in 1951. This role involved fostering international cooperation, developing programs abroad, and supporting foreign visitors. The bureau was central to the university's engagement with global political and cultural issues during the Cold War.

During my tenure at UC Berkeley, I worked on initiatives related to the Peace Corps, helping to develop programs that promoted international understanding and service. This was part of broader efforts to engage students and faculty in global affairs during the Cold War era. The Peace Corps represented a practical application of international cooperation.

At Berkeley, I collaborated with the Institute of International Studies to support international visitors and develop academic programs. This partnership helped bridge scholarly research and practical diplomacy during the Cold War. Together, we addressed challenges related to immigration, communism, and cultural exchange.

My academic and professional work during the 1950s was deeply influenced by the Cold War. At UC Berkeley, I navigated issues of communism, immigration, and international cooperation through teaching and program development. The Cold War context shaped much of the university's and my own engagement with global affairs.
